hook: You already know how to solve a system of two linear equations like $2x_1 + x_2 = 5$ and $x_1 - 3x_2 = -1$ by substitution or elimination. But what happens when you have fifty equations and fifty unknowns? Carrying out row operations on that many equations written out longhand would be a nightmare. Can we compress an entire system of linear equations into a single, compact object that a computer — or you — can work with efficiently?
concept: The matrix equation $Ax = b$ is a way to rewrite an entire system of linear equations as a single equation involving a matrix and two vectors. If $A$ is an $m 	imes n$ matrix of coefficients, $x$ is an $n 	imes 1$ column vector of unknowns, and $b$ is an $m 	imes 1$ column vector of constants, then $Ax = b$ encodes every equation in the system simultaneously. The product $Ax$ is defined as the linear combination of the columns of $A$ weighted by the entries of $x$, and setting that equal to $b$ asks: what weights make those columns combine to hit $b$?
anatomy: In $Ax = b$, there are four distinct components worth naming precisely. The coefficient matrix $A$ (of size $m 	imes n$) stores all the coefficients of the unknowns, with each row corresponding to one equation and each column corresponding to one variable. The solution vector $x = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ \vdots \\ x_n \end{bmatrix}$ holds the $n$ unknowns you are solving for. The right-hand side vector $b = \begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ \vdots \\ b_m \end{bmatrix}$ holds the constant on the right side of each equation. Finally, the matrix-vector product $Ax$ is computed as a linear combination: $Ax = x_1 a_1 + x_2 a_2 + \cdots + x_n a_n$, where $a_j$ denotes the $j$-th column of $A$, making consistency of dimensions ($A$ must have $n$ columns to match the $n$ entries of $x$) non-negotiable.

worked_example.description_latex: We start with a concrete system of three equations in three unknowns. Our goal is to rewrite this system in the compact form $Ax = b$, which means we need to identify and separate out the coefficient matrix, the unknown vector, and the right-hand side vector.

worked_example.expression_latex: A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 3 & 2 \\ -1 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad x = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad b = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix}
worked_example.description_latex: We form $A$ by reading off the coefficients row by row — the coefficient of $x_1$, $x_2$, $x_3$ in each equation becomes one row of $A$. Notice that the order of variables must be consistent across every row, and any variable that is missing from an equation contributes a $0$ in the corresponding position.

worked_example.expression_latex: Ax = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 3 & 2 \\ -1 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2x_1 + x_2 - x_3 \\ x_1 + 3x_2 + 2x_3 \\ -x_1 + x_2 + x_3 \end{bmatrix}
worked_example.description_latex: Here we verify that the matrix-vector product $Ax$ actually recovers the left-hand side of each original equation: the $i$-th entry of $Ax$ is the dot product of the $i$-th row of $A$ with $x$, which is exactly the left-hand side of the $i$-th equation. This step confirms that the translation from system to matrix form is faithful.

worked_example.expression_latex: \begin{bmatrix} 2x_1 + x_2 - x_3 \\ x_1 + 3x_2 + 2x_3 \\ -x_1 + x_2 + x_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix} \iff Ax = b
worked_example.description_latex: Setting $Ax$ equal to $b$ enforces all three equations simultaneously, because two vectors are equal if and only if every corresponding entry is equal. So the single equation $Ax = b$ is not a shortcut that loses information — it is completely equivalent to the original system.

partial_example.description_latex: We want to write this two-equation system in the form $Ax = b$. Start by identifying the coefficient of each variable in each equation so we can build the matrix $A$ and the vectors $x$ and $b$.

The coefficient matrix and vectors are given above. Now verify that the matrix-vector product $Ax$ reproduces the left-hand side of both equations by computing $Ax$ explicitly as a column vector — write out each entry as a dot product.

Compute $Ax$ by taking the dot product of each row of $A$ with $x$, and write the result as a column vector. Then confirm that setting this equal to $b$ gives back both original equations. What is $Ax$?

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Write the system $x_1 + 2x_2 = 3$, $4x_1 - x_2 = 5$ in the form $Ax = b$. Identify $A$, $x$, and $b$.
Given $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 2 \\ -1 & 3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ and $b = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$, write out the system of linear equations that $Ax = b$ represents.
Verify that $x = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$ is a solution to $Ax = b$ where $A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$ and $b = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 10 \end{bmatrix}$.
practice_problems.answer_latex: Ax = \begin{bmatrix} 3(1)+(-1)(2) \\ 2(1)+4(2) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 10 \end{bmatrix} = b. \text{ Yes, } x \text{ is a solution.}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Write $Ax = b$ in augmented matrix form $[A \mid b]$ and use row reduction to determine whether the system is consistent, given $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$, $b = \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ -6 \end{bmatrix}$.
practice_problems.answer_latex: \left[\begin{array}{cc|c} 1 & -1 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]. \text{ Consistent; infinitely many solutions: } x_1 = 3 + x_2, \ x_2 \text{ free.}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A small factory makes two products. Product I requires 2 hours of machining and 1 hour of finishing; Product II requires 1 hour of machining and 3 hours of finishing. The factory has 8 machining hours and 9 finishing hours available. Write a matrix equation $Ax = b$ modeling this situation, where $x_1$ and $x_2$ are the quantities of each product.

common_mistakes: Students often build $A$ by reading columns instead of rows — placing all coefficients of $x_1$ in the first row instead of the first column. Remember that each ROW of $A$ corresponds to one equation, and each COLUMN corresponds to one variable.

When a variable is missing from an equation (for example, $x_1 + x_3 = 5$ with no $x_2$ term), students frequently omit that position in $A$ entirely, shifting all later coefficients left. You must always insert a $0$ as a placeholder so every column consistently represents the same variable.

Students sometimes set up $Ax = b$ with a row vector $x^T$ instead of a column vector $x$, making the dimensions incompatible. The product $Ax$ requires $x$ to be an $n \times 1$ column vector, so that an $m \times n$ matrix times an $n \times 1$ vector yields an $m \times 1$ vector.
untested_variants: Systems where $A$ is not square (more equations than unknowns, or fewer equations than unknowns), requiring discussion of overdetermined or underdetermined systems.
Expressing $Ax$ as a linear combination of the columns of $A$ weighted by the entries of $x$, and using that column-combination view to reason about whether $b$ lies in the span of the columns.
Homogeneous systems $Ax = 0$: identifying the solution set, understanding the difference between trivial and nontrivial solutions, and connecting it to the null space of $A$.
Using the matrix equation framework with block matrices or partitioned matrices, where $A$ is expressed as a block and $x$ is partitioned accordingly.
